evidence: Multiple modules model dependencies as very large function-field seam structs plus manual nil-validation (e.g., `RectifyRuntime` + `mergeRectifyRuntime` + `validate` in `goseg/rectify/rectify.go`, `Runtime*Ops` families in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go`, `importerRuntime` and `validateImporter*` in `goseg/importer/importer.go`).

The same seam-heavy shape propagates into tests as large setup/stub scaffolding (`testWireguardRuntime` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/wireguard_test.go`, `testDockerRoutineRuntime` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/docker_test.go`, startup runtime builders in `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator_test.go`).

Config mutation is split across two parallel abstraction systems: field-by-field merger logic (`goseg/config/config_merge.go`) and generic patch registry (`goseg/config/config_update_registry.go`), both encoding overlapping domain fields and policies.

Broadcast mutation is represented by a transition abstraction (`BroadcastTransition`, `ApplyBroadcastTransition` in `goseg/broadcast/transitions.go`) but other paths mutate and publish state directly (`updateBroadcast` in `goseg/leak/broadcast.go`, direct state helpers in `goseg/broadcast/state.go`), weakening the abstraction boundary.

Section-scoped mutation APIs rely on `any` and runtime casting across config and orchestration boundaries (`structs.SysConfig.UpdateSection`, `config.UpdateUrbitSectionConfig`, `RuntimeUrbitOps.UpdateUrbitSectionFn`).

Parallel registry/mapping tables encode overlapping section/domain wiring in multiple places (`SysConfigSectionDescriptors`, `sysConfigSectionPatchSpecs` + `confPatchFieldsForSection` switch, and orchestration section aliases), increasing drift risk.

Transition handlers in shipworkflow combine policy decisions, external side effects, and persistence updates in single functions (e.g., startram toggle/endpoint/minio-link flows), reducing separable ownership.

Session lifecycle state is split into multiple mutable collections that require manual synchronization (`authClients`/`authTokens`, `sysLogSessions`/`sysSessionsToClear`) with cleanup paths that do not enforce strong invariants.

Config patch registry construction mixes panic-based failures in goseg/config/config_update_registry.go (configPatchFieldFromSpec panics on invalid spec) with error-returning registry validation in goseg/config/config_update_schema.go (buildConfPatchByKey/buildConfigPatch return errors).

WiFi runtime service methods mostly use explicit error returns for invalid receiver/runtime state, but StopWiFiInfoLoop in goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go has no nil guard and no error contract, while adjacent methods return errors on nil receivers.

Ship lifecycle status retrieval is handled inconsistently: goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go propagates GetShipStatus failures, but goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go (urbitCleanDelete) suppresses initial status lookup errors and proceeds with deletion.

findings.summary: Runtime dependency abstraction is over-fragmented into many callback bags, creating high indirection with limited behavioral leverage.
evidence: `RectifyRuntime` composes multiple nested ops structs and then uses long merge/validate chains (`mergeRectifyRuntime`, `validate`) instead of a smaller cohesive runtime contract.
`runtime_ops.go` declares many narrowly sliced ops structs (`RuntimeContainerOps`, `RuntimeSnapshotOps`, `RuntimeUrbitOps`, etc.), increasing seam surface and call indirection.
`importerRuntime` has three nested runtime groups with many function callbacks and dedicated validation errors for each missing seam, indicating seam-management overhead has become a primary concern.
`main.go` repeats similar callback-runtime wrappers (`bootstrapCoordinator`, `bootstrapRuntimeFns`, `bootstrapRuntime`) that mostly forward calls plus nil checks.
findings.suggestion: Collapse callback-bag seams into a smaller set of cohesive capability interfaces per workflow boundary (for example, startup orchestration, importer provisioning, rectify reconciliation), and keep function-field seams only where tests truly need per-function override. Prefer constructor-injected concrete adapters over per-call forwarding wrappers.

findings.summary: Test harnesses mirror production seam fragmentation, requiring large synthetic runtimes that signal abstraction cost outweighs testability benefits.
evidence: `testWireguardRuntime()` builds a broad runtime object with many nested callback groups before each behavior can be tested.
`testDockerRoutineRuntime()` in subsystem tests initializes a large set of function seams (container/network/http/timer/broadcast) even for focused behavior tests.
Startup tests rely on helper mutation of nested runtime internals (e.g., `unsetStartupInitConfig`) to exercise validation paths, indicating high ceremony around seam plumbing.
Main tests construct custom nested runtimes (`c2cRuntime` and bootstrap runtime wrappers) for unit scenarios that could be simpler with narrower ports.
findings.suggestion: Introduce task-focused test fixtures around narrower ports (for example, one interface per use-case) and reduce globally shared mega-runtime shapes. This will lower setup friction and make abstraction boundaries reflect behavior instead of callback inventory.

findings.summary: Configuration mutation logic is duplicated across two abstraction models, creating a split domain boundary and drift risk.
evidence: `config_merge.go` hardcodes per-field merge behavior across many `SysConfig` fields.
`config_update_registry.go` defines a generic field-spec registry for patch parsing/application over many of the same config domains.
Both files encode domain policy for overlapping fields (connectivity/runtime/startram/auth/penpai), but through different abstractions and code paths.
Large config tests in `config_test.go` validate many merge-policy branches, reflecting policy concentration in one path while registry path evolves separately.
findings.suggestion: Converge on a single configuration policy abstraction: either drive merge behavior from the registry metadata or define a single domain-policy layer that both patch application and merge operations call. Remove duplicate per-field policy encoding.

findings.summary: The broadcast transition abstraction is bypassed by direct state mutation/publish paths, making the intended abstraction non-authoritative.
evidence: `goseg/broadcast/transitions.go` defines `BroadcastTransition` plus centralized apply helpers intended as mutation boundary.
`goseg/leak/broadcast.go` mutates outbound broadcast shape and publishes scoped/full payloads directly via channels, outside transition contracts.
`goseg/broadcast/state.go` exposes direct state update helpers (`UpdateBroadcast`, scheduling/state mutators), allowing mutation paths that bypass typed transitions.
`rectify_services.go` manipulates broadcast-related state and emits transition updates through service-specific logic, reinforcing multiple mutation pathways.
findings.suggestion: Make transition/mutation entrypoints authoritative by routing all broadcast state changes through a unified mutation API (typed transitions plus explicit mutation commands), and limit direct state setters to internal package scope where possible.

findings.summary: Section mutation contracts are untyped across module boundaries, forcing runtime checks and silent mismatch paths.
evidence: `SysConfig.UpdateSection(section, mutateFn any)` dispatches via descriptor `mutate` functions that type-assert and no-op on mismatch.
`UpdateUrbitSectionConfig` and `UrbitSectionMutator` accept `mutateFn any` and only validate shape at runtime.
`RuntimeUrbitOps.UpdateUrbitSectionFn` propagates this `any` contract into orchestration APIs.
findings.suggestion: Replace `any` mutator plumbing with typed section-specific interfaces or a generic `UpdateSection[T]` API returning explicit errors on type mismatch; keep one typed adapter at boundary edges instead of propagating dynamic typing through core runtime structs.

findings.summary: Config section/domain wiring is duplicated across independent registries, creating coherence drift risk.
evidence: `SysConfigSectionDescriptors` defines section access/mutation in `structs`.
`sysConfigSectionPatchSpecs` plus `confPatchFieldsForSection` separately map section-to-patch behavior.
Orchestration re-exports Urbit section constants and wires section update callbacks again, adding another mapping surface.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single canonical section manifest (section id, typed accessor, patch fields, runtime exposure) and generate/construct per-layer adapters from that manifest so new sections cannot be added in one layer without compile-time breakage in others.

findings.summary: Startram/Urbit transition functions mix orchestration policy, side effects, and persistence in monolithic flows.
evidence: `runStartramToggleWithRuntime` decides policy (enable/disable), manipulates containers, dispatches ship payloads, and schedules rebuild/load steps in one routine.
`runStartramEndpointWithRuntime` combines stop/unregister/key rotation/config writes in one transition chain.
Urbit operations (e.g., boot/minio toggles) similarly combine config reads, external calls, and persistence in per-command handlers.
findings.suggestion: Split transition handlers into composable use-cases: pure state-transition planning (policy), side-effect executors (docker/startram/click), and persistence adapters. Keep handlers as thin orchestrators over these components to reduce responsibility mixing and enable focused testing.

findings.summary: Session management duplicates authority across parallel collections, making lifecycle invariants easy to violate.
evidence: `ConnectionRegistry` stores both client buckets and token sets; `removeConnectionByConn` updates buckets but does not reconcile token sets.
Auth checks use different authorities (`hasAuthToken` token map vs `hasAuthSession` active client scan), so state can diverge.
`logstreamSessionStore` uses separate active and pending-removal slices requiring two-phase calls (`AddSysSessionToRemove` then `RemoveSysLogSessions`).
findings.suggestion: Define one authoritative session model per subsystem and derive secondary views on read. Centralize cleanup transitions in a single mutation path that enforces invariants (e.g., remove connection => recompute token presence atomically).

findings.summary: Large hand-built fixtures are repeated across tests, coupling tests to full config/runtime shape rather than intent-level helpers.
evidence: `config_test.go` repeatedly constructs very large `defaultCfg/customCfg` objects via long field-by-field assignment blocks.
`startup_orchestrator_test.go` manually assembles nested runtime callback structs in many tests.
`importer_test.go` includes multiple ad-hoc runtime/config helper patterns (`importerRuntimeWith`, `authorizeTokenIDsForTest`, reset/setup helpers), indicating fixture logic spread.
findings.suggestion: Create shared test builders (e.g., `NewSysConfigFixture().With...`, `NewStartupRuntimeFixture()`) with sensible defaults and narrow override methods, then reuse across modules to keep tests focused on behavior instead of structural assembly.

findings.summary: Config patch registration uses panic for invalid specs while sibling paths return recoverable errors
evidence: configPatchFieldFromSpec panics when apply/target accessors are missing (lines 19-23), causing process-level failure instead of returning an error.
The same config update pipeline otherwise models invalid registry state as regular errors (buildConfPatchByKey returns error; buildConfigPatch wraps confPatchByKeyErr).
findings.suggestion: Replace panic checks in configPatchFieldFromSpec with error-returning constructors (or validate at buildConfPatchByKey time) so malformed registry definitions fail through the same returned-error path as other config patch validation errors.

findings.summary: WiFi service API mixes explicit errors with panic-prone/no-error methods on adjacent operations
evidence: Most methods return explicit errors for nil receiver/runtime state (IsWifiEnabled, ConstructWifiInfo, ToggleDevice, ApplyCaptiveRules), but StopWiFiInfoLoop dereferences service/state without nil checks and cannot return an error.
ConstructWifiInfo always returns nil after RefreshInfo call because RefreshInfo has no error channel, creating a success return even if refresh internals fail.

Tests explicitly cover nil-receiver error behavior for ConstructWifiInfo (TestConstructWifiInfoNilReceiver) but there is no equivalent error contract for StopWiFiInfoLoop, reinforcing inconsistency in failure semantics.
findings.suggestion: Standardize WiFi service failure contracts: add nil-guarded behavior for StopWiFiInfoLoop (or return error), and refactor RefreshInfo path to return/propagate refresh failures so ConstructWifiInfo can report operational errors rather than only setup errors.

findings.summary: Container deletion path suppresses status lookup failures that sibling lifecycle paths propagate
evidence: urbitCleanDelete ignores the initial getShipRunningStatus error via `if err == nil { ... }` and proceeds to DeleteContainerFn even when status retrieval fails for reasons other than ship absence.

In toggleBootStatus, ship status lookup errors are immediately returned with context, including not-found sentinel wrapping, showing a stricter and different failure policy for equivalent status dependency.
findings.suggestion: Make status lookup handling explicit in urbitCleanDelete: propagate non-not-found status errors before destructive operations, and only continue delete flow for explicitly accepted conditions (e.g., not-found). Reuse shared helper/policy for ship-status error classification across shipworkflow paths.
